The central argument is that Mamism emerges when anger is no longer merely used or imitated, but believed in. Under Mamism, anger becomes a communicative ideology. Anger is reinterpreted as instruction, intimidation as leadership, public correction as education, silence as discipline, and fear as productive tension. As a result, resistance to anger may be interpreted as weakness, immaturity, irresponsibility, or disruption of order. Mamism thereby produces organizational silence, reduces psychological safety, distorts emotional currency, legitimizes harassment, selects for anger-adaptive actors, and devalues non-anger-based communication. The paper argues that escaping Mamism requires more than emotional regulation training or anti-harassment education. Organizations must reduce the symbolic and practical value of anger within workplace communication, refuse to treat anger as evidence of competence or responsibility, and create conditions in which dialogue, explanation, repair, humor, psychological safety, and non-anger-based control are organizationally valued. The problem is not anger itself, but the communicative and ideological structure through which anger becomes believed in as organizational morality.

articles.title: Fantasy Engagement & Sleep quality among Binge-watchers
articles.abstract: Watching multiple episodes for a long period using different streaming platforms such as Netflix, Viki, Wetv, Hulu, has become widespread in recent years. The interest in Korean pop music, dramas and movies have also increased widely among youth during the recent years. There is much attention paid to Binge-watching behaviour, but its relations to Fantasy Engagement and Sleep quality among K-content watchers during Covid-19 has dearth of research. So, this present study sought to fill this gap by investigating the sleep quality and fantasy engagement of college students who binge-watch K-contents during the covid-19 pandemic. Purposive sampling method was used in the study, and a sample of 100 female adult respondents were collected. Binge-Watching Engagement and Symptoms Questionnaire (BWESQ), Sleep Quality scale (SQS) and Fantasy Engagement scale were used to assess the variables. To find the relationship and difference between the variables, Pearson’s product moment correlation and independent t-test were used. The findings of the study indicated that high Binge-watching behaviour leads to Fantasy Engagement and acute sleep problems.

articles.title: The evolution of punk: From Rebellion to Retail
articles.authors: Anika Dhingra, Gokul Prabhu, Ananta Ganjoo
articles.abstract: As a working-class subculture with strong political roots, the punk ideology emerged in the 1970s in opposition to establishment politics and consumer culture. Thus, at first, punk was both a cultural revolution and a tool for political self-expression. This article analyzes how the politicized punk subculture turned into a commercial aesthetic phenomenon that operates in today’s capitalist society. Using the period approach, this review article analyzes punk’s development from an underground phenomenon of the 1970s, to its politicization in the 1980s, massification in the 1990s, diversification in the 2000s, and reinvention in the digital era of the 2010s and 2020s. The primary focus is on showing how media images, globalization, and the fashion industry transformed the visual elements of punk culture into mere icons divorced from their ideological background. Theories such as “Subcultural Zoo” explain the mechanisms employed by capitalistic economies in the process of appropriation of subcultures, thus turning their distinctive traits into standardized commodities. Additionally, the study points out the socio-economic and moral consequences of such a transformation, notably, the marginalization of working-class subjects and the use of oppressive modes of production. Regardless of its commodification, this paper asserts that the essence of punk ideology remains alive in alternative settings, especially through grassroot efforts and online forums. In the end, the study defines punk ideology as a living and ever-changing concept with different interpretations.

articles.title: Who Overreports? Regime Support and Preference Falsification in Iran
articles.authors: Daniel L. Tavana, Kevan Harris, chi shun fong, Amir Farmanesh
articles.abstract: In authoritarian contexts, individuals often express greater support for the regime in public than they hold in private. This gap---overreporting---is typically treated as a measurement problem rather than a political behavior to be explained. We argue that overreporting deserves greater attention in survey-based studies of regime support and is itself shaped by which social referents are salient to respondents during survey interviews. We develop and test an original theoretical framework that identifies three sources of overreporting: expressive identification, behavioral conformity, and civic resources. Drawing on a pre-registered, nationally representative telephone survey of residents of Iran (N=1,999) with an embedded double list experiment, we estimate regime support from the list experiment at 47 percent---roughly ten percentage points below the 57 percent recorded by direct questioning. Our findings indicate that expressive identification matters most: national pride and religiosity both predict substantial overreporting. Preference falsification in Iran is shaped less by the coercion citizens face from the state than by the identity-based attachments the regime encourages---findings with implications for how scholars measure and interpret regime support in closed societies.

articles.title: Personality Emergence and Adaptive Functioning: An Enactive, Complex-Dynamical Systems Account
articles.abstract: Enactive psychiatry conceptualizes mental disorders as maladaptive modes of functioning that emerge within the recursive coupling between an embodied agent and their world. This chapter advances an integrative framework synthesizing enactive principles with complex dynamical systems theory to illustrate how personality operates as the foundational architecture of these functional modes. Personality is conceptualized as an evolving topography of attractor landscapes or stylistic regularities, structuring multi-level sense-making across nested timescales. Fundamental to this account is adaptive flexibility, the metastable capacity of a system to balance structural coherence and environmental exploration. The multi-level topology of individual differences is mapped through the traits and facets of the Five-Factor Model, with developmental pathways traced to early temperament and interaction patterns, reframed here as sedimented relational topologies of structural coupling. Personality difficulties or maladaptive functioning is understood as global alterations in control topology, characterized either by pathological rigidity and high inertia, or systemic fragmentation and high instability. Finally, this framework operationalizes enactive theory through an empirical turn, utilizing real-time tracking methodologies and time series analyses to capture intrapersonal and interpersonal coordination. Clinical change is comprehended as context sensitive perturbations that facilitate systemic reorganization, providing a conceptually coherent and clinically actionable science of the person.

articles.title: Risky Business: On the politicisation of risk in European AI standardization
articles.abstract: Technical standards are supposed to become a key material infrastructure and regulatory mecha-nism in global AI governance. Nonetheless, there are only few empirically grounded studies, researching the inherent interest politics and value conflicts within the design and development process of AI standards. Drawing on fieldwork conducted between 2023 and 2026, including interviews with standardisation experts, regulators, and standards-developing organisations, analysis of standards documents and reports, and participant observation in committees and online communities, this paper examines how AI is translated into technical standards and con-structed as a regulatory object. In contrast to approaches that emphasise the depoliticising effects of standardisation practices, the findings reveal an emerging politicisation of the field. Rather than merely implementing regulatory objectives, standardisers engage in divergent conflicts over competing visions of AI. Empirically, the paper identifies three classification conflicts concerning categorisation, measurement, and interference. While risk serves as a central organising principle for classifying AI systems, its practical enactment remains contested and the standardisation process itself remains risky as its outcomes are uncertain and contested. By outlining these conflicts, this paper inter-venes in the growing yet heterogeneous debate on AI governance in practice. The findings suggest that ongoing conflicts within AI standardisation may significantly influence the future implementation, legitimacy, and effectiveness of EU AI governance.

articles.title: What the North Can Learn from the South Sex Work, Self-Organisation, and Extraterritorial Power in the Global South. A Theoretical Synthesis.
articles.abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work in the Global South and argues for an inverted learning gradient. Where an earlier companion paper showed that in the Global North the future of sex work is decided in labour law and in digital and financial infrastructure that the state would have to provide, this paper shows that in the Global South the protective infrastructure exists where sex workers have built it themselves - as peer organisation, cooperative banking, and community health networks, often before and without the state. Drawing on peer-reviewed research and movement scholarship - including Mgbako's fieldwork on African sex worker activism, the literature on the Sonagachi project and the Durbar collective in India, research on the Latin American network RedTraSex, and Agustin's analysis of the 'rescue industry' - the paper makes three claims. First, the degree of self-organisation, not the legal framework, is the strongest predictor of protective capacity in the South. Second, the same extraterritorial US power that regulates the North through platform law operates in the South through funding and visa policy, exemplified by the PEPFAR anti-prostitution pledge, the 2012 visa denials that forced an alternative AIDS conference to Kolkata, and the 2025 dismantling of PEPFAR itself - which strikes hardest at precisely those structures that had made themselves dependent on it. Third, the learning gradient between North and South runs in both directions: the South can learn institutional permanence from the North, the North can learn self-built infrastructure and peer efficiency from the South. South Africa's ongoing decriminalisation process serves as the Southern counterpart to the Belgian experiment. Related work https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/uc57b_v1

articles.title: Data-driven Chinese investment and trade in Europe beyond Huawei and TikTok
articles.abstract: Data access and related security implications have become a frequent concern in Chinese companies’ global expansion. This paper argues that the intensifying debate over the national security implications of Chinese companies’ data access abroad lacks a more nuanced understanding of the types of data accessed. Using a typology of four data types: transaction, product, process, and transmission and storage data, access to data by Chinese companies in Germany is empirically investigated. The analysis shows that venture capital investments, mergers and acquisitions, greenfield investments, and trade provide access to different types of data. Trade is the most important internationalization strategy for access to data, and product data is the most frequently accessed data type. This has several implications for the debate over the security risks posed by Chinese companies’ access to data in Europe. Trade requires little physical presence in Germany; data access can be achieved without ownership of large-scale physical infrastructure. It reveals a mismatch between the predominant regulatory focus on mergers and acquisitions and trade as the primary strategy for data access, which is less regulated. Trade, as the central data access strategy, also implies that much depends on the market success of China’s products and services.

articles.title: Exploring the mobilities of Kenyan older people living in informal settlements using a capability approach
articles.authors: Carlo Luiu, Amos Wandera, Vera Bukachi, Rosie Day
articles.abstract: Despite significant discussions on transport inequalities in African cities, older people's mobility needs remain under-researched. To gain a deeper understanding of such issues, this study focuses on older people living in Kenyan informal settlements and uses the capability approach to investigate their options for mobility, activities and locations, barriers, coping strategies and unfulfilled mobility needs. The study employs a qualitative approach comprising four focus groups with 24 participants and a follow-on community engagement workshop with people aged above 50 years old living in the informal settlement of Kibera in Nairobi. The results indicate that older people face significant challenges in their everyday mobility. Living with poor health conditions, level of poverty, lack and poor quality of walking infrastructure, personal safety and ageist behaviour are the main factors shaping and affecting their mobility. The capability approach offers a framework to understand older people’s mobility practices and implications for wellbeing. It enables an analysis of how mobility is shaped by their needs, constraints and decision-making processes, and how this translates into participation in life. It also offers an exploration of how mobility enables and supports a wider capability set, outlining its multidimensional social impacts.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: The Scarcity Theory of Specialization: Polymathy, the Architecture of Knowledge, and the Question Posed by Artificial Intelligence
articles.abstract: Modern science policy, professional training, and the research university share a foundational assumption: excellence requires specialization. This article argues that the assumption rests on a category error. Specialization is a rational adaptation to the cognitive scarcity of individual human minds, not an intrinsic property of high-quality cognition, and a civilization that mistakes the adaptation for the ideal will misread both its intellectual history and its technological present. Drawing on the economics of innovation, studies of creative eminence, and the sociology of science, the article develops a scarcity theory of specialization and tests it against three bodies of evidence: the recombinant structure of major discoveries, the documented liabilities of deep expertise, and the avocational breadth of eminent scientists. The article then disaggregates specialization into three distinct functions, cognitive economy, social coordination, and epistemic certification, and argues that large-scale artificial intelligence dissolves only the first. Foundation models constitute a natural experiment demonstrating that breadth and depth can be jointly scaled when memory and time constraints are relaxed. Four falsifiable propositions and implications for doctoral education and research funding follow. The deeper principle organizing knowledge, the article concludes, is not division but integration.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: When the Standard Becomes the Shield: SA 600, Group Audit Failures, and the Case for Convergence with ISA 600 in India
articles.abstract: This paper examines whether, and how, the design of India's group auditing standard, SA 600 (2002), shaped the interpretive conditions in which audit deficiencies arose in corporate failures, and evaluates the case for convergence with ISA 600. The paper draws on documentary analysis of fifty NFRA enforcement outputs — audit quality review reports (AQRRs) and disciplinary orders issued between 2019 and 2024 across six public interest entities — supplemented by NFRA meeting minutes, the regulator's 2024 consultation on a revised standard, and thirty-two comment letters. The analysis is situated against the international literature on group audit quality and divided auditor responsibility. NFRA's enforcement record reveals a consistent pattern: principal auditors treated SA 600 (2002)'s division of responsibility clause as a basis for limiting their engagement responsibility for subsidiaries and associates, even where fraud indicators were visible. The dominant failures were of implementation — non-compliance with requirements SA 600 already imposed — rather than of design; yet the standard's design structured the interpretive environment so that non-compliance became predictable and defensible. The Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI) did not submit a response to NFRA's public consultation, which effectively constitutes an institutional impasse; while most respondents supported revision, practitioners were divided. Although NFRA's enforcement outputs have featured in Indian professional commentary, they have not previously been mobilised as a systematic empirical corpus in the group audit literature, and this paper is among the first to do so. It demonstrates that standard divergence can create interpretive conditions in which non-compliance becomes institutionally defensible. This has implications for jurisdictions weighing whether and how far to converge national group auditing standards with international requirements on auditor responsibility.

articles.title: Are the children of homeowners increasingly advantaged? Trends in relative and absolute mobility of homeownership in Europe
articles.authors: Selçuk Bedük, Enrico Benassi, Philipp M. Lersch
articles.abstract: Homeownership is declining among younger generations across Europe. A common assumption is that this decline has been increasingly stratified by parental homeownership, due to rising house prices and the growing importance of parental financial support. We show that this assumption does not hold for the average European (and for most countries). Using data from EU-SILC 2011 and 2019 covering 24 European countries, we examine trends in relative and absolute mobility of homeownership for birth cohorts 1951 to 1984. On average, relative mobility remained largely stable across cohorts, while absolute mobility increased. The apparent stability of relative mobility masks a deeper structural cohort shift: downward mobility increased from 14 per cent to 38 per cent, while upward mobility declined from 23 per cent to 8 per cent between cohorts born in the early 1950s and early 1980s. Therefore, homeownership has declined significantly for both children of homeowners and renters. We also compare absolute and relative mobility trends across countries and show that they often diverge for two reasons. First, a mechanical effect of the origin distribution: in countries with widespread parental homeownership, relative mobility largely reflects changes in upward mobility. Second, the source of mobility: using two novel counterfactual simulations, we show that observed absolute mobility changes mainly stem from structural shifts in homeownership chances, rather than strengthening parent-child associations.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Mathematics of Forever Wars
articles.abstract: In this paper, we use a popular game-theoretical model, called the Game of Chicken, to generate a random walk in the plane that seems to model well the forever wars of the present, and of the past. The main point of the paper is to show that simple game-theoretical models have a lot of predictive power. The second point is that quantum game algorithms may offer solutions to social problems humanity was not yet able to see.

articles.title: Gender, Populist Attitudes and Voting Behaviour: A Critical Revisitation of Spierings & Zaslove (2017) in Light of EES 2014–2024 Evidence
articles.abstract: This article provides a systematic critical reassessment and empirical update of Spierings and Zaslove's (2017) seminal study on the gender gap in populist voting, integrating a decade of subsequent scholarship and comparative evidence from the European Election Studies (EES) of 2014 and 2024. The original work posited that female socialization toward consensus and conflict aversion structurally distanced women from the antagonistic rhetoric of populist parties, particularly those of the populist radical right (PRR). Drawing on nine-country EES 2014 data and a fifteen-country EES 2024 dataset, we demonstrate that the aggregate PRR gender gap has narrowed considerably — from an average of −5.7 percentage points in 2014 to −3.7 points in 2024 — while exhibiting pronounced cross-national heterogeneity. We attribute this convergence to three partially overlapping mechanisms: (1) the strategic de-demonization of PRR parties under female leadership, producing a 'maternal-authoritarian' aesthetic that lowers women's psychological costs of voting radical right; (2) the emergence of femonationalism as a gendered discursive register that reframes nativist and anti-immigration stances as pro-women positions; and (3) a generational re-sorting, whereby the gender cleavage has migrated from the general electorate to Generation Z, where male respondents support PRR parties at rates 15 percentage points above their female counterparts, while young women increasingly anchor the populist radical left (PRL). These findings challenge the passive socialization model and call for analytical frameworks that treat female agency as constitutive of contemporary populism rather than merely reactive to it.

articles.title: Orphaned Knowledge: A Custodial Theory of Authorship for the Age of Artificial Intelligence
articles.abstract: Scholarly publishing and intellectual property law have converged on a similar rule for generative AI: a machine cannot be an author because it cannot be accountable, and outputs without a human creator do not fit established regimes of protection or credit. This article argues that these responses are sound but conceptually incomplete because they treat authorship mainly as a matter of origination and reward. Drawing on Foucault, Merton, Shapin, and Hardwig, I argue that authorship has long bundled three functions: origination, reward, and custodianship. Generative AI disrupts that bundle by separating origination from the forward-looking human work of defending, developing, teaching, remembering, and answering for knowledge over time. The result is what I call orphaned knowledge: findings that may be true and valuable yet lack any identifiable person committed to keeping them alive. The article makes two contributions. First, it shows that both the credit economy of journals and the property economy of copyright are structured around human knowers, leaving machine-originated knowledge in a double vacuum. Second, it advances a custodial theory of authorship that redefines the author’s signature less as a retrospective claim to credit than as a prospective commitment to steward a finding. On this view, a machine may originate, but only a human or institution can undertake custody. The age of machine discovery therefore forces scholarship to confront a prior question that earlier authorship debates have obscured: not only who made knowledge, but who will keep it.

subjects.name: Social and Philosophical Foundations of Education

articles.title: An inductive-deductive circular methodology of the research process: Systematic thinking from prior assumptions to final synthesis
articles.abstract: In this essay, we reflect on the circularity of social science research and how we can attempt to put this circularity into a system to help structure our thinking and research practices through structured inductive-deductive reflection. We present our model for thinking of research as a holistic process from making our prior assumptions concrete and updating them systematically through constant comparison and a circular inductive-deductive analysis, towards a synthesis of the research findings.

articles.title: Contained and Uncontained Experiments
articles.abstract: I introduce a typology of political science experiments based on their ``containedness." Contained experiments are those for which it is plausible to ``perform the same procedure" and expect ``the same result." Uncontained experiments are those for which practitioners expect intrinsic variation to dominate; even when performing the same procedure, the goal is to understand how results differ across time and context. I propose three conceptions of containedness: pragmatic (community expectations about replication), complexity (information required to specify the procedure), and temporal (speed of experimentation relative to drift in the phenomenon). Lab experiments are generally the most contained, survey experiments occupy a middle position, and field experiments are the least contained. I provide a diagnostic rubric for evaluating containedness and argue that distinct evidentiary strategies are appropriate at different levels. For highly uncontained experiments, ``replication" is not a coherent goal; knowledge accumulation must proceed through theory rather than induction.

articles.title: Genetic Mirroring in Adoptees: Toward a Formal Construct Definition, Nomological Network, and Operationalization Framework
articles.abstract: Genetic mirroring, the developmentally recurring need to perceive reflected biological similarity in genetically related others, has gained clinical salience as an explanatory framework for the identity experiences of adoptees and donor-conceived individuals. Despite this, the construct lacks the theoretical architecture required for empirical investigation: a precise definition, differentiation from adjacent constructs, and a plausible operationalization pathway. The present paper addresses that gap through four coordinated contributions. First, a formal construct definition is proposed, encompassing three theoretically distinct dimensions: biological salience, embodied discontinuity, and mirroring responsiveness. Second, the construct is situated within a nomological network specifying its hypothesized relationships with established psychological variables, including identity exploration, narrative identity coherence, attachment security, genealogical bewilderment, and physical self-concept. Third, a theoretical case is made for incremental validity over existing frameworks: genetic mirroring is proposed to capture variance in adoptee psychological functioning that neither attachment theory nor narrative identity frameworks can fully account for. Fourth, a preliminary scale structure is proposed as proof of concept. The paper's aim is to transform a clinically emergent construct into a formally theorizable and measurable entity, enabling the empirical program necessary to evaluate its scientific and practical significance.

articles.title: Hedging Under U.S. Reindustrialization: Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia in an Era of Intensifying U.S.–China Rivalry
articles.abstract: Southeast Asian states maintain deep economic ties with both the United States and China, and the region has become a major destination for supply-chain relocation from firms in both powers. Recent U.S. reindustrialization policies—including the extension of elevated tariffs to Southeast Asian exports—pose new challenges to the region's export-led growth model and to U.S. strategic positioning in the Indo-Pacific. Drawing on hedging theory, particularly Cheng-Chwee Kuik's distinction between return-maximizing and risk-contingency behaviors, this paper examines how three manufacturing-oriented, trade-dependent economies—Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia—adjusted their foreign-policy and geoeconomic orientations in response to successive waves of U.S. reindustrialization from 2017 to 2026. Using a theory-driven event-coding approach, the study analyzes 66 verified foreign-policy and geoeconomic events to trace directional tilts toward Washington, Beijing, or neutrality across three U.S. administrations. The findings show that U.S. reindustrialization has not uniformly pushed Southeast Asian states toward China. Instead, different phases of U.S. policy produced qualitatively distinct hedging responses, mediated by structural factors that vary across countries. The analysis also reveals a misalignment between U.S. tariff instruments and the goals of the Indo-Pacific Strategy: tariff shocks create openings for Chinese economic leverage in the very economies U.S. industrial policy seeks to draw closer. The durability of the Indo-Pacific Strategy will depend on whether Washington can align its economic tools with its security ambitions and provide the structural conditions that enable partners to hedge coherently under intensifying great-power competition.

articles.title: eHealth Use among Adults with Chronic Illness in the United States: Health Self-Efficacy and Health Literacy as Pathways to Mental Well-Being
articles.authors: Qianfeng Lu, Wen Jiao, Qiaofei Jovi Wu
articles.abstract: Patients with chronic illness face a considerable mental health burden. However, the pathways through which eHealth use influences mental health in this population remain poorly understood. Drawing on Street's (2003) three-stage model, this study examined the effects of eHealth use on mental health among chronic illness patients, with health self-efficacy as a mediator and health literacy as a moderator. Data from the Health Information National Trends Survey 7 (HINTS 7) were analysed among U.S. adults with chronic illness (N = 2,928) using the PROCESS macro. The direct effect of eHealth use on mental health was insignificant. eHealth use was negatively associated with health self-efficacy, which was then positively linked to mental health. Health literacy moderated the relationship between eHealth use and health self-efficacy, with the negative association significant at lower literacy levels but diminishing and trending positive at higher literacy levels. Consequently, the indirect effect of eHealth use on mental health through health self-efficacy was contingent on health literacy, being negative at lower levels and diminishing as literacy increased. These findings suggest that health literacy determines whether eHealth use burdens patients with chronic illness. Therefore, ensuring adequate patients health literacy is important in eHealth interventions targeting this population.

articles.title: Human Brokers in Machine Times: The Network Architecture of Journalism from COVID-19 to the AI Era
articles.authors: James Jaehoon Lee, Jeffrey Layne Blevins, Jeremy D. Gilbert, Joe Germuska
articles.abstract: The dominant narrative of journalism in the digital age has focused on institutional decline and audience fragmentation. However, during the COVID-19 pandemic, we find that news organizations surprisingly reemerged as central actors within digital information ecosystems, serving as network brokers between fragmented online communities. Five years later, we conducted a 6,000-respondent cross-national survey to show that news-mediated brokerage remains durable in the era of AI models, but individual content creators share the brokerage role through which distinct publics come into contact. Here we perform a longitudinal analysis of the changing role of news organizations from 2020 to 2025, by combining network analysis of 141 million tweets from 24 million users during 2020, 45 qualitative interviews with younger news consumers across the United States, Nigeria, and India, and a 6000 person YouGov survey fielded in June 2025 across the US, UK, India, Nigeria, Brazil, and South Korea. Using network analysis and the concept of brokerage, we show that journalism operates as a connective layer in network architecture, channeling information flow across ideologically and geographically distinct communities. We identify a platform-native model of journalism that departs from traditional article formats in favor of contextualized, multimodal, and shareable content. Our findings suggest that in the era of digital platforms, the conventional journalistic product of the article gives way to a distributed model of sense-making, in which journalism's value lies in brokering connections rather than delivering monolithic event narratives. By 2025, the brokerage function persists but the brokers themselves have shifted. Individual creators now share the bridging labor that news organizations performed alone in 2020. These findings suggest a new account of journalism as a dynamic connective broker within networked public life and contemporary information ecosystems.

articles.title: bqmm: Bayesian Multilevel Quantile Regression in R
articles.abstract: Quantile regression describes how covariates shift the conditional quantiles of an outcome, not merely its mean, and is indispensable when effects are heterogeneous across the response distribution. When data are clustered or longitudinal, a mixed-effects formulation is needed. I present bqmm, an R package for Bayesian multilevel quantile regression built on the asymmetric Laplace working likelihood and Stan. The package offers an lme4-style formula interface with nested and crossed random effects, optional LKJ-correlated random effects, estimation of one or several quantiles in a single call, post-hoc non-crossing rearrangement, and a transparent menu of fixed-effect interval methods — the naive posterior, the Yang–Wang–He (2016) sandwich correction, and the infinitesimal jackknife — because the asymmetric Laplace likelihood is misspecified and naive credible intervals can be invalid. The paper describes the model and software design, illustrates usage on longitudinal growth data, summarises a validation study (parameter recovery, simulation-based calibration, and a coverage study), and compares bqmm with related software.

articles.title: Beyond Individual Vulnerability: A European, Critical Work and Organizational Psychology Perspective on Work Addiction in the Digital Workplace.
articles.abstract: Adopting a European vantage point, both empirically and with respect to the regulatory context, this position paper argues that work addiction in digitally mediated work cannot be adequately understood as a matter of individual vulnerability or as a mechanical effect of technology. Drawing on behavioural-addiction models and on critical work and organizational psychology, it advances a psychosocial and practice-oriented reading in which compulsive work emerges from the interaction of personal dispositions, organizational cultures, role demands, digital tools, and recognition systems. The paper distinguishes work addiction from work engagement and from heavy work investment, locating the construct in the combination of excess and compulsion rather than in working hours. It then reframes digital tools (email, messaging, collaborative platforms, and the smartphone) not as neutral instruments but as facilitators, amplifiers, and maintenance environments, connecting the argument to constructs the field has already named, including workplace telepressure, low psychological detachment, technostress, and the autonomy paradox. The argument is extended to the still-fragmented, partly worker-led adoption of artificial intelligence at work, drawing on European and OECD evidence that points to task intensification rather than wholesale displacement, and to governance that lags adoption. A central claim is that organizations frequently reward continuous availability as professionalism, normalizing compulsion as a virtue and obscuring it as a psychosocial risk. Against this backdrop, recent European and Italian developments on the right to disconnect are read as encouraging but partial signals. The paper closes with propositions for a critical-practice agenda, framing the right to disconnect as an organizational competence rather than an individual hygiene practice.

articles.title: Public perceptions of climate policy fairness and effectiveness across climate policy types and domains in 15 countries
articles.authors: Nina L. Frings, Ulf J.J. Hahnel
articles.abstract: Individuals are more supportive of climate policies when they think the measures are fair and effective. This evidence, however, is primarily based on data from Western samples, leaving it open how public effectiveness and fairness perceptions vary across geographical contexts and how they are shaped by regional indicators. Here, we aim to address these questions, investigating climate policy effectiveness and fairness perceptions across four policy types and two policy domains in 15 countries, covering all continents (N=6’052). Effectiveness and fairness ratings varied between policy types and domains, and this pattern was relatively stable across nations. However, individuals in the Global South were overall more optimistic about the effectiveness of climate policies and evaluated them as generally fairer compared to the Global North. Linking psychological and contextual data further shows that effectiveness and fairness perceptions were higher in countries that are more vulnerable to climate change impacts and lower in countries with higher per capita emissions. Moreover, the impact of psychological variables such as climate change concern and political orientation on both outcomes varied as a function of national context. Taken together, our findings point to important geographical asymmetries in the extent to which the public perceives climate mitigation policies as effective and fair, shaped by how much countries contribute to and are affected by climate change.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: What voters think and feel about a climate law: cognitive–affective mental models during a Swiss national referendum
articles.authors: Nina L. Frings, Julius Fenn, Samuel Aeschbach, Rui Mata, Ulf J.J. Hahnel
articles.abstract: Lacking public support remains a major barrier to ambitious climate policy, yet little is known about how citizens mentally represent specific policy proposals and whether or how these representations change over time. We assessed voters’ cognitive– affective mental models of a real-world climate policy proposal, the 2023 Swiss Climate Protection Law, at two time points preceding a national referendum on the policy. A sample of Swiss voters (N=254) drew their mental models by adding their own cognitive associations and affective evaluations towards the law one month before and immediately prior to the referendum. Opposers were more likely to make reference to financial or personal costs and to rate concepts more negatively, whereas supporters were more likely to make reference to climate and future protection concepts and to rate concepts more positively. While the cognitive associations differed across voter groups, the complexity of mental models, indicated by the number of provided concepts and their interconnectedness, did not. Both opposers and supporters reported more cost-related concepts at the second time point but there was no strong evidence of increased complexity or changes in affective evaluations over time. Crucially, affective evaluations were the strongest predictor of final policy support relative to cognitive associations. These results suggest that while political campaigns may shape the considerations citizens associate with a policy, policy support does not necessarily reflect differences in the complexity of these representations and may be more closely associated with their affective evaluation than with their cognitive content.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Upfront Pricing or In-Game Purchases? Monetizing Content in Video Games
articles.abstract: A video game developer can monetize its content (maps, themes, modes) in two ways: upfront pricing, a single high up-front price for the game with all content included, or in-game purchases, a free or cheap base with each content piece sold separately. We model this as a choice between bundling and separately selling a content stream and ask when in-game purchases dominate. Upfront pricing wins when content tastes are dispersed and weakly correlated; in-game purchases win when tastes are strongly correlated or pieces diﬀer in appeal, when risk-averse players face uncertain content, and when players are present-biased or impulsive. A simulation with all forces active shows that bundling is the benchmark optimum and that each friction shifts the boundary toward in-game purchases, with behavioral forces moving it most. The framework explains why premium console and PC games are still sold up front, while long-lived games, especially mobile games, rely on in-game purchases.

articles.title: Benchmarking the Personalization Capabilities of Large Language Models
articles.authors: Vinay Aggarwal, Yaman K Singla, Ashutosh Srivastava, Siddharth Yedlapati, Shashwat Dixit, Jitendra Ajmera, Balaji Krishnamurthy
articles.abstract: Personalization, the act of varying a message to induce action from a specific receiver while keeping sender, channel, and time fixed, has a long tradition in psychology and marketing as a two-party problem in which sender and receiver have independent objectives. Large language models remove the bounded-inventory constraint of classical retrieval-and-ranking approaches by generating a continuum of message variants conditioned on inferred receiver state, raising the question of how well current models perform personalization in the classical sense. Existing LLM personalization benchmarks measure sender-side adaptation, in which the receiver is the same user the model is serving. The two-party question, whether a generated message induces its intended action in a third party, has been investigated only through A/B tests and small-scale human studies that cannot be re-run against a new model on demand. We adapt the Bayesian Persuasion framework of Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011) to generative agents and instantiate the formulation in sales, where receiver actions are routinely logged against the outreach that induced them. We release SDR-Bench, a public corpus of 6,279 customer success stories spanning 22 industries and approximately 200 enterprises, served through a temporally constrained simulation that prevents future-data leakage. Across frontier LLMs and deep-research agents, we observe a consistent personalization plateau and on a Fortune 100 tech cohort no model statistically separates successful from unsuccessful outreach. A field deployment with 12 professional sales representatives validates the framework, with 48 percent of model-generated content rated immediately useful and senior-expert agreement at Pearson 0.82. We release SDR-Arena and SDR-Bench publicly to support reproducible study of generative personalization at scale.

articles.title: When, and how much, might effect estimates be biased when analysing mixed-mode survey data? The roles of mode effects, mode selection, and mode split.
articles.authors: Georgia D Tomova, Richard J. Silverwood, Liam Wright
articles.abstract: Survey data are increasingly collected using mixed-mode designs (e.g. personal interview and web questionnaire). Little is known, however, about the extent to which this introduces bias in subsequent analyses, or whether simply including mode as a covariate addresses it. Using data simulations, we identified the conditions under which mode effects (mode measurement differences) and mode selection (respondent differences) introduce bias, complementing this with empirical illustrations using mixed-mode data from the 1958 National Child Development Study. In simulations, absent mode selection, substantial bias arose only from unusually large mode effects, but was amplified when mode selection was present. Controlling for mode under strong mode selection introduced substantial bias, including sign-reversal of the estimate. Bias was more pronounced when the sample was split equally between modes. The direction and size of bias will depend on the direction of all effects. In our empirical illustration, the results were largely unchanged after controlling for mode, possibly reflecting weaker mode selection and mode effects in these data. These findings highlight that, while possible, substantial bias from mixed-mode designs may be unlikely in many practical settings. However, the risk of bias, and the appropriate strategy to address it, should be considered on an analysis-specific basis.

articles.title: Buying time, doing family? Domestic outsourcing and time use in Sweden
articles.authors: Betiel Selassie, Elien Dalman, Lisa Eklund, Therese Nilsson
articles.abstract: Domestic outsourcing is often presented as a way to reduce unpaid work, ease work-family conflict, and promote gender equality. However, it remains unclear whether outsourcing domestic services changes how individuals divide their time between paid work, housework, childcare, and other forms of unpaid work. This paper examines the relationship between domestic outsourcing and time use in Sweden, a strong dual-earner context in which the RUT tax deduction subsidizes half of domestic services costs. We link the 2021 Swedish Time Use Survey to administrative register data on household-level use of RUT services to reliably capture outsourcing incidence and intensity. This linkage allows us to examine how domestic outsourcing associates with time spent on paid work and types of unpaid work, and how these associations differ by gender and parenthood status. The results indicate that outsourcing associates with differences in time allocation above a relatively high threshold of regular use, but that the relationship between outsourcing and time use depends on family type. Regular domestic outsourcing associates with a pattern consistent with time allocation theory among individuals without resident children. Non-parenting men and women who regularly outsource spend substantially more time on paid work and less time on unpaid work than otherwise similar non-outsourcers. Reductions in unpaid work are concentrated in routine housework, the activity most directly substitutable. Among parents, by contrast, regular outsourcing does not associate with more time in paid work nor clear reductions in total unpaid work. Mothers show some reduction in routine housework, accompanied by increases in non-routine housework, while fathers in regularly outsourcing households do not spend less time on unpaid work, including routine housework. The results suggest that domestic outsourcing cannot be understood simply as buying time for paid work. Among parents it may supplement rather than replace own unpaid work and may thus help sustain intensive combinations of employment, caregiving and other parts of domestic life. The analysis also reveals discrepancies between register-based and self-reported measures of outsourcing. The study contributes to research on gender, outsourcing, and work-family dynamics by showing that the meaning of “buying time” varies by gender and parenthood.

articles.title: Inclusive by design: a three-pillar framework for equitable online conferences
articles.authors: Ebi Antony George, Natacha Rossi, Alexis Buatois, Valentin Lecheval
articles.abstract: Scientific conferences remain central to scholarly communication, yet participation is constrained by several systemic barriers. Although online conferencing expanded rapidly during the COVID-19 pandemic, there remains limited evidence regarding which organisational practices effectively support equitable participation and sustained engagement. Here, we examine Animal Behaviour Live (ABL), a multi-year international online conference series, as a case study in inclusive conference design. We propose a framework structured around three organisational pillars – who participates, who presents, and who organises – combined with an iterative evaluation of participant engagement. For each pillar, we identify systemic barriers to inclusion and discuss targeted organisational strategies derived from our experience across four conference editions. Synthesising demographic data, platform analytics, and simulations of abstract selection outcomes, we show that ABL sustains broad international participation and long-term asynchronous engagement while increasing representation across geographic regions, gender, and career stages. We further demonstrate that a continent-stratified abstract selection process actively mitigates psychological self-selection biases, such as a gendered confidence gap in format preference. This process also outperformed random or score-only selection approaches in simultaneously balancing scientific quality, gender representation, geographic diversity, and inclusion of researchers self-reporting lower access to the international scientific community. Our findings establish that equitable representation in online conferences does not emerge by default and provide an evidence-informed framework with actionable strategies for designing more inclusive international scientific conferences.

articles.title: Was der Norden vom Süden lernen kann Sexarbeit, Selbstorganisation und extraterritoriale Macht im Globalen Süden. Eine theoretische Synthese.
articles.abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work in the Global South and argues for an inverted learning gradient. Where an earlier companion paper showed that in the Global North the future of sex work is decided in labour law and in digital and financial infrastructure that the state would have to provide, this paper shows that in the Global South the protective infrastructure exists where sex workers have built it themselves - as peer organisation, cooperative banking, and community health networks, often before and without the state. Drawing on peer-reviewed research and movement scholarship - including Mgbako's fieldwork on African sex worker activism, the literature on the Sonagachi project and the Durbar collective in India, research on the Latin American network RedTraSex, and Agustin's analysis of the 'rescue industry' - the paper makes three claims. First, the degree of self-organisation, not the legal framework, is the strongest predictor of protective capacity in the South. Second, the same extraterritorial US power that regulates the North through platform law operates in the South through funding and visa policy, exemplified by the PEPFAR anti-prostitution pledge, the 2012 visa denials that forced an alternative AIDS conference to Kolkata, and the 2025 dismantling of PEPFAR itself - which strikes hardest at precisely those structures that had made themselves dependent on it. Third, the learning gradient between North and South runs in both directions: the South can learn institutional permanence from the North, the North can learn self-built infrastructure and peer efficiency from the South. South Africa's ongoing decriminalisation process serves as the Southern counterpart to the Belgian experiment. Related work https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/df43j_v1

articles.title: Politics of Association and Evaluative Logics in the Climate Contrarian Blogosphere
articles.abstract: This study provides an investigation of the processes of legitimation and evaluative logics of the climate contrarian blogosphere. Attention is a scarce resource in information rich environments. Drawing on theoretical frameworks from Bourdieu’s Field Theory and Science and Technology Studies, hyperlinks are conceptualised as offering an insight into the enacted epistemological and axiological preferences of climate sceptic bloggers as these digital objects trace who is deemed worthy of attention and what kinds of knowledge practices these targeted sources represent. Data was collected from the blogrolls of 198 climate sceptic and lukewarmer blogs that were sampled by a snowball method to generate a final dataset of 857 nodes/websites with 2628 edges/hyperlinks. These nodes were thematically classified to identify types of websites and sources. Analysis of network composition showed the most prominent sources on the climate contrarian blogosphere were climate sceptic sources (35.1%), right-wing sources (16.7%), and scientific sources (12.3%). Further node-level models and exponential random graph models were employed to estimate how enacted preferences differed by structural position and influenced tie formation processes. This paper contributes to sociological theories of legitimation by demonstrating how evaluative logics in digital fields of struggle can be reconstructed through the relational traces of enacted preference.

articles.title: Data Centres and the Emergence of the Twin Transition Industrial Complex
articles.authors: Giorgos Bougioukos, Charis Papaevangelou, Eugenia Siapera
articles.abstract: The EU has called the ‘twin transition’ a win-win strategy for tackling climate change and strengthening its digital autonomy amid geopolitical and geoeconomic tensions. However, high-level policy discourse can hide the material frictions, extractive dynamics, and emerging dependencies associated with implementing this agenda. The paper examines Western Macedonia, a region historically defined by lignite extraction, now transforming into Greece’s post-lignite development hub. Drawing on critical data centre studies and critical political economy scholarship, we trace the emergence of a twin transition industrial complex (TTIC), that is a system of a parallel and interlinked development of renewables and large-scale, often AI-ready data centres. Methodologically, the study combines 12 semi-structured interviews with locals, on-site visits, as well as an analysis of relevant policy and corporate communication documents. We discern four key findings: A) Western Macedonia is transformed into a hub for renewable energy and data centres, which will help Greece integrate into the global AI value chain. B) However, this shift is causing the displacement of locals, especially young people, and creating a ‘limbo’ situation that will lead to social and ecological exhaustion. C) The green transition is also creating ‘green enclosures’ and transforming the landscape by converting land, including historically publicly funded PPC land, into assets for a more exclusionary and extractive digital transformation. D) Despite official narratives portraying Western Macedonia as a ‘Greek Silicon Valley’, locals are excluded from decision-making and governance processes, leading to disillusionment and a sense of hopelessness for the future. Our main contribution is the outlining of an emerging twin transition industrial complex, wherein the twin transition and digital transformation are planned and materialised by state and (Big Tech) capitalist actors according to the imperatives of the AI industry’s global value chains at the expense of local communities and ecosystems.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Naturalistic measure of social norms alignment
articles.authors: Yevhen Kostiuk, Kenneth Enevoldsen, Peter Bjerregaard Vahlstrup, Márton Kardos, Kristoffer Nielbo
articles.abstract: Social norms reflect shared expectations on acceptable behavior. Measuring social norms alignment remains challenging, with existing approaches typically relying on artificial closed-form evaluations such as multiple-choice questionnaires or measuring agreement with predefined statements. In the context of this work, social norms alignment refers to measuring an agreement between solutions with respect to the social problem or dilemma. We propose a framework for measuring social norm alignment in naturalistic, free-form settings through solution matching. The framework enables us to measure alignment between any two dilemma responses e.g., LLMs to a human, LLMs to LLMs, or human to human. We introduce two metrics: stated and explicit agreement accuracy, and construct a dataset of 3k non-trivial social dilemmas in Danish. All dilemmas are assigned reference solutions derived from three panelists, who serve as culturally grounded judges. We evaluate the agreement of several LLMs and human responses in an interaction setup that resembles natural user-model conversations. Our results show that the proposed metrics produce consistent model rankings and reveal variation in agreement across different types of dilemmas, with higher agreement observed for topics such as neighbor conflicts and shared living situations. Overall, our work introduces a dataset and evaluation framework for studying culturally grounded social reasoning in naturalistic open-ended conversations.

articles.title: Personality traits predict (not) seeking professional help for common mental health problems
articles.authors: Helo Liis Soodla, Kirsti Akkermann, Kadri Kõiv, Kelli Lehto, Estonian Biobank research team, Uku Vainik, René Mõttus
articles.abstract: Personality traits may explain why many people with clinical-level mental health concerns do not seek professional help. Among 52,784 participants in the Estonian Biobank, we examined the personality profiles of individuals experiencing significant depression and anxiety disorder symptoms, comparing those who did and did not seek help, as recorded in a nationwide diagnostic register. We also assessed traits’ overall predictive accuracy for help-seeking. Help-seekers tended to exhibit trait profiles more characteristic of psychopathology. For example, even after adjusting for self-reported depression and anxiety disorder symptoms, they had higher neuroticism and openness, and they specifically reported being more stress-prone and feeling unhealthy. Personality traits substantially predicted help-seeking, with nuances (Area Under Curve, AUC = 0.71) outperforming domains (AUC = 0.63), self-reported psychopathology (AUC = 0.65) and demographic factors (AUC = 0.54). Quick, non-invasive personality trait assessments could help detect those less likely to seek help and contribute to early, targeted interventions.

articles.title: Using agent-based simulations to anticipate risks and mitigation measures in platform governance
articles.authors: Max van Drunen, Natali Helberger, Aytalina Kulichkina, Annie Waldherr
articles.abstract: Risk analysis and mitigation in platform governance are constrained by the difficulties of testing changes to platforms’ systems and accessing data. Other fields, ranging from public health to economics, as well as an emerging body of platform research, use agent-based modeling to overcome this obstacle by empowering researchers and regulators to test changes virtually. However, it is still unclear how and under what conditions agent-based modeling can effectively support platform governance. This article aims to reduce this uncertainty in two ways. First, it provides a theoretical framework on the benefits and risks of using agent-based modeling in platform governance, drawing on literature on platform risk assessment, the use of agent-based modeling in other fields, and anticipatory governance and evidence-based policymaking. Second, to validate this framework and clarify the conditions under which regulators can rely on evidence produced by agent-based models, the article reports the results of interviews with 18 regulators. The article finds that agent-based simulations have significant potential to complement other sources of evidence used in platform risk assessment. Their main added value is that they allow researchers and regulators to more easily, ethically, and independently explore the potential impacts of changes to platform design, and provide interactive and visual scenarios that concretize abstract platform dynamics.

articles.title: “I even saw four deer grazing”: online-offline entanglements in the psilocybin pluriverse
articles.authors: Michał Wanke, Paweł Matuszewski, Piotr Siuda
articles.abstract: Purpose This paper aims to examine evolving practices and discourses surrounding psilocybin mushrooms on an online drug forum. Situating use within hybrid digital and ecological contexts, it traces shifts in user engagement before and after the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic to contextualize localized realities of the contemporary psychedelic renaissance. Design/methodology/approach Using an agential cut framework, the authors conducted a thematic analysis of approximately 3,000 qualitative posts from the largest Polish forum (mid-2000s–2023). The study tracks how forum users integrate digital tools (mapping, smartphone verification) with environmental field observations, mapping the online-offline entanglements and more-than-human relations of foraging. Findings Analysis reveals a post-2020 shift from immersive, metaphysical narratives toward predictive, scientifically informed and risk-managed practices. Building directly on critical drug scholarship regarding assemblage thinking and ontological multiplicity, the authors conceptualize the forum as an apparatus within a psychedelic pluriverse, where radically different versions of psilocybin simultaneously coexist, negotiate legitimacy and sustain themselves. Originality/value By integrating longitudinal forum analysis with a multispecies and pluriverse perspective, the study shows how digital platforms and embodied ecological practices jointly shape contemporary psychedelic practices, highlighting their relational, contingent and ethically informed character and demonstrating how shifts in discursive framings coincide with changing modes of ecological attention and risk reflexivity.

articles.title: The role of altmetrics in citation emergence: a temporal approach
articles.authors: José Luis Ortega, Evangelina Becerra Rodero, Sergio Castro Cortacero
articles.abstract: This study aims to identify differences in the temporal distribution of altmetrics, detect associations between them, and determine which metrics have the greatest capacity to influence citation impact. Mentions and their appearance dates for 7,963 research publications were extracted from Altmetric.com. Several data mining techniques (transition matrices and association rules) and survival analysis methods (the Kaplan-Meier estimator, Cox proportional hazards models, and Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) models) were used to find longitudinal relationships between altmetrics and bibliographic citations. The results showed that there are two groups of metrics according to their temporal distribution. Attention metrics (social networks and digital media) are those that occur almost immediately after publication and have a short lifespan, while publication metrics, which come from edited publications (citations, Wikipedia, policy documents, patents), appear later and accumulate gradually over time. Social networks and digital media have greater interactions among themselves than publication metrics. Regarding the role of altmetrics in preceding citation impact, social networks have the ability to trigger the appearance of citations and shorten the time it takes for them to occur. Specifically, Research Highlights and Clinical Guidelines, two metrics associated with qualitative assessments, are those that most strongly influence the appearance of citations.

articles.title: Consent to sharing shopping data for health research: a mixed-methods study
articles.authors: Romana Burgess, Poppy Taylor, Kate Shiells, Mark Mumme, Andy Boyd, Anya Skatova
articles.abstract: Introduction: Shopping data offer detailed, objective records of diet and lifestyle habits, providing a valuable but under-used resource for health research. While willingness to share personal data has been widely studied, no previous research has directly compared stated willingness with subsequent actual data sharing within the same cohort. Understanding whether willingness predicts actual consent, and how this varies by sociodemographic characteristics, motivations, and barriers, is essential to improve recruitment strategies and the representativeness of collected data. Methods: We employed a mixed-methods approach using the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). Quantitative analyses combined sociodemographic variables with responses from a 2018 survey assessing willingness to consent to shopping data linkage and outcomes from a 2023 loyalty card consent campaign. These were complemented by semi-structured interviews in 2019 exploring attitudes towards sharing shopping data. Results: In 2018, 60.7% of participants indicated willingness to share shopping data (n = 2142, 9230 invited). In 2023, actual consent—requiring provision of loyalty card details for five UK retailers (Sainsbury’s, Tesco, Boots, Morrisons, and Coop)—was obtained from 511 of 6170 invited participants. Demographic characteristics were broadly comparable across samples. Stated willingness was associated with higher odds of actual consent (adjusted OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.16–1.90), indicating that willingness is a meaningful predictor of subsequent data sharing. Sociodemographic characteristics and motivations (e.g., societal benefit, scientific interest) showed modest associations with both willingness and consent and moderated the willingness–consent relationship. Interviews reinforced the importance of trust in ALSPAC and clear communication about data use, while highlighting privacy concerns as minor barriers. Conclusion: This study provides the first within-cohort evidence that willingness to share shopping data predicts subsequent data sharing, albeit imperfectly. Willingness surveys can therefore inform recruitment planning, but additional strategies are required to improve realised consent rates. Addressing practical barriers, clarifying data use, and highlighting societal and scientific benefits may help narrow this gap; Targeted engagement, including through informative materials and outreach events, may help to reach groups less likely to consent. These insights can inform data collection in longitudinal cohorts and wider population samples.

articles.title: Data Note: Linking Shopping Transaction Data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children
articles.authors: Romana Burgess, Mark Mumme, Andy Boyd, Anya Skatova
articles.abstract: Introduction: This data note describes the collection and linkage of participant’s Sainsburys shopping data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). Methods: Data were collected via informed participant consent and Subject Access Requests under UK GDPR, securely transferred and integrated into the ALSPAC database using unique study identifiers. Participant consultation and a technical pilot ensured transparent, acceptable, and secure linkage. Results: The dataset contains anonymised transactions for 244 participants, covering 658,375 item purchases between July 2014 and February 2024. Transactions include an item description, price, quantity, time, date, store location, and other variables. Items fall into 82 categories (e.g., produce) and 1002 subcategories (e.g., bananas). In terms of representativeness, the cohort is skewed towards females, consists of participants of similar age, and is predominantly made up of individuals with higher educational attainment and of White ethnicity. Conclusion: The linkage of shopping records complements ALSPACs extensive existing dataset, which comprises over 30 years of sociodemographic and health-related data. The linkage stands as a novel opportunity for future research into dietary habits, nutritional intake, and subsequent health outcomes. Access to data may be granted to approved researchers upon reasonable request, following a proposal submission through the ALSPAC website (http://www.bristol.ac.uk/alspac).

articles.title: AI in the Loop?! A Systematic Review of the Use of Large Language Models in Survey and Public Opinion Research
articles.authors: Leah von der Heyde, Florian Keusch, Trent D Buskirk, Adam Eck
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly transforming the entire pipeline of the survey and public opinion research process. The complexities of LLMs and the magnitude of their anticipated disruption warrant the specific attention of the field, but existing overviews are non-systematic, narrow, or theoretical. In this systematic, comprehensive review of the empirical literature, we investigate how LLMs are used before, during, and after data collection. We conduct a quantitative and qualitative assessment of 136 studies, synthesizing at which tasks, contexts, and research designs LLMs succeed and fail and which applications are underexplored. We find that up until 2025, research has mainly focussed on classifying text data, generating survey data, and developing survey instruments. Although there is a large diversity of possible models and interaction approaches, most research designs apply single models of the GPT family with zero-shot prompting to English-language contexts, putting the generalizability and replicability of individual studies into question. Nevertheless, patterns emerge: Across applications, LLMs perform better when approximating broad, well-represented aggregate patterns than nuanced individual attitudes, topics, or constructs. LLMs can be considered as supporting humans rather than replacing them. Going forward, careful model choices, reporting standards and survey-specific benchmarks would propel the field towards more generalizable use cases. Although the rapid technological development conflicts with rigorous qualitative assessments, survey methodologists are well-positioned to adopt LLMs, which will continue to depend on human-curated input and validation data. With this review, we expand the understanding of the potentials, concerns, and gaps of LLM use in survey and public opinion research, providing a starting point for future innovations. We make our thoroughly coded database available for future use and expansion.

articles.title: Following Socio-Environmental Conflict Narratives About Energy Transition in Chile: A Spatio-Temporal Analysis Using Dynamic Topic Modeling
articles.authors: Jonas Rieger, Felipe Muñoz, Lars Grönberg, Kai-Robin Lange, Iván Ojeda-Pereira, Dario Briceño, Christian Nass, Carsten Stahl, José Cassola, Carolina Rojas-Córdova
articles.abstract: Understanding the construction of socio-environmental narratives at a national scale is a complex challenge, particularly when research remains fragmented across disconnected case studies. In Chile, the energy transition has generated territorial disputes as extractive industries and renewable energy projects expand, yet large-scale systematic analyses of how these conflicts are represented in public discourse remain scarce. This paper addresses this gap by applying a spatio-temporal topic modelling framework to a corpus of 1,996 validated news articles covering conflicts related to the energy transition in Chile from 2011 to 2025. Using RollingLDA, a dynamic adaptation of latent Dirichlet allocation that prevents information leakage from future documents, we identify twelve topics that provide insights into the public narratives surrounding socio-environmental conflicts. Our analysis reveals how specific conflicts, such as the HidroAysén dam project, the Dominga mining controversy, and pollution in sacrifice zones such as Quintero-Puchuncaví, have evolved over time, with some narratives declining while others, including green hydrogen development and lithium extraction, have emerged as central concerns. We complement this temporal analysis with a spatial dimension by mapping the prevalence of topics across Chilean regions through an interactive dashboard. By combining established methods, our work offers a reproducible framework that can be adapted to topic modelling results incorporating spatial and temporal dimensions, enabling the tracking of how socio-environmental narratives emerge, evolve, and fade over time. Please also refer to the GitHub repository at https://github.com/JonasRieger/t2s2026.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Experiences of Mental Health Support Services in a Community Sample of Parents of Children with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities
articles.abstract: Parents of children with special educational needs and disabilities (SEND) face numerous additional challenges on top of everyday parenting that place them at higher risk of mental health difficulties. However, poor mental health in parents of children with SEND could potentially be alleviated by appropriate and timely targeted mental health services. This study examined the experiences of mental health and mental health service provision in a community sample of parents living in and around the city of York, England. Data from 66 survey respondents (89.4% female) and 12 interviewees (67% female) were thematically analysed. Results indicated that parents experienced numerous challenges related to navigating complexity in their day-to-day lives and in accessing service provision for their children. Systemic limitations in service accessibility were identified, with participants frequently relying on informal networks and charitable organisations for support due to insufficient or inappropriate formal provision. For some, services were experienced as ineffective or harmful. The absence of appropriate external support for children’s needs placed a substantial burden on parents, contributing to psychological distress, feelings of isolation, and parents neglecting their own needs to provide for their children. Participants called for more integrated, strengths-based, and family-centred approaches, particularly flexible parallel provision for parents and children. These findings highlight the need for systemic reform to ensure that SEND support services are accessible, joined-up, and responsive to both child and parental needs.

articles.title: Analysis of language diversity on islands requires adequate statistical modelling - a comment on Bromham, Yaxley and Cardillo (2024)
articles.abstract: In a recent study, Bromham, Yaxley and Cardillo (BYC) assembled an impressive database of 1,197 languages spoken across 13,100 islands to examine how predictions from island biogeography theory (IBT) apply to linguistic diversity and whether islands act as drivers of language change. I commend the authors for the significant effort in compiling such a truly global dataset of island languages, and for quantitatively demonstrating that islands hold a disproportionately greater share of the world’s languages than expected based on their land area. However, I here contend that the core model used to test the predictions of IBT and the hypothesis that islands shape language evolution is empirically implausible and plagued by several critical issues.

articles.title: Physician-Mentors’ Experiences of Generativity, Burnout, and Work-Life Balance: A Pilot Study
articles.authors: Atticus Chance Emig, Kate De Medieros, Woods Nash
articles.abstract: According to Erik Erikson, generativity involves investing oneself in future generations, and mentoring is one way to achieve generativity.1 In academic medicine, the mentoring of medical students and residents is an essential means by which the profession self-perpetuates, with each generation of mentees inheriting and adapting their mentors’ knowledge, skills, and behaviors to how they teach, conduct research, and care for patients. Despite the paramount importance of physicians’ mentorship of future medical professionals, existing literature focusing on generativity as it relates to mentorship in academic medicine is sparse. This pilot study evaluates physician educators’ perceptions of generativity and their career trajectory around four foci: people (e.g., mentees), systems (e.g., medical training), activities, and career outcomes. In 2024, 10 participants were recruited via a Health Humanities listserv to participate in a brief survey and comprehensive hour-long interview. Findings suggest that, for physician-mentors, mentorship is protective against burnout by increasing their feelings of worthiness and work satisfaction. These professionals desire mentorship to be appreciated as an integral facet of medical practice deserving of financial investment and recognition by their institutions. Additionally, participants felt that fostering a culture of ethics and compassion was as important as teaching technical skill. Participant reports of institutional barriers to attentive patient care were correlated with higher scores for burnout and exhaustion. In conclusion, generative mentorship is highly applicable for organizational investment and investigations of fidelity and ethics in education.

articles.title: Fracture Without Failure: The Politics of Collective Action in Diverse Workplaces
articles.abstract: How do diverse societies achieve shared political goals despite intergroup divisions? Attempts to overcome diversity’s challenges for collective action often focus on improving attitudes through contact, yet cooperation also depends on strategic relations. I examine this question using U.S. service-sector labor union organizing campaigns, a high-stakes political process in which interaction across diverse workers on shared shifts is routine and observable. Using a national survey of hourly workers and administrative campaign records, I find that diversity is associated with uncertainty and disagreement about union support that sustained contact does not resolve. Evidence from a pre-registered experiment shows that diversity causes more risk-averse strategic behavior, and that this relationship is not mediated by interpersonal attitudes. I conclude that contact targets interpersonal attitudes without suﬃciently reducing perceptions of strategic risk. At the same time, diversity is not associated with closer union election margins, suggesting that institutional structures may help workers overcome coordination problems and inviting further study.

articles.title: Battlefield dynamics and the timing and location of terrorism in civil conflicts
articles.abstract: Knowing when and where terrorism occurs during civil conflicts is of both scholarly and practical importance. Previous research showed that battlefield losses shape the timing of terrorism and that some administrative regions are more prone to terrorist activity than others. However, these approaches rarely explain the timing and location of terrorist attacks jointly or how these patterns may vary by target type. This paper argues that short-term changes in conflict intensity, measured by the number of clashes or the resulting government and rebel casualties, systematically shape when and where rebels resort to terrorism. Building on a signaling and tactical substitution framework, I show that battlefield dynamics affect both the likelihood of terrorism and its spatial concentration and target choice.
