Using geographically and temporally disaggregated data from civil conflicts in Africa at the grid cell–month level and generalized linear hierarchical models, the analysis demonstrates that terrorism clusters near recent battle sites and regional political centers, with distinct patterns following rebel versus government losses. These findings advance our understanding of terrorism as a reactive tactic in civil conflicts and contribute to the broader literature on disaggregated conflict dynamics and political violence.

articles.title: Negative ties offset complex contagion effect on malaria preventive measure adoption in a hard-to-reach population
articles.authors: Elisa Bellotti, Federico Bianchi, Francesco Renzini, Carinthia Balabet Nengnong, Anna Maria van Eijk, Anne Kessler, Rajiv Sarkar, Jane M. Carlton, Sandra Albert
articles.abstract: Interpersonal influence within social networks plays a central role in the diffusion of health behaviors, with trusted peers and opinion leaders often providing the social reinforcement necessary for adoption in contexts where legitimacy or trust is limited. However, the countervailing role of negative ties—characterized by dislike, mistrust or conflict—remains understudied. This study examines how negative ties can offset positive peer effects in the adoption of a malaria preventive measure—an insecticidal cream—in a hard-to-reach village in Meghalaya, India. We analyzed the adoption process using sociocentric signed network data collected from 102 villagers. Results show that on average adoption was most likely when at least three trusted peers used a cream, but that the presence of even one negative tie to an adopter reduced the probability of adoption to baseline levels, offsetting the positive influence of multiple trusted peers and of the local healthcare providers. These findings provide rare empirical evidence of behavioral distancing driven by negative contacts and highlight how negative relationships can disrupt complex contagion processes. Public health interventions designed for hard-to-reach populations warrant the incorporation of negative ties and of relationships with opinion leaders into diffusion models.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Local Pachamama Beliefs and the Expansion of Cooperation among the Amazonian Kichwa
articles.authors: Connor Wood, Lexi Carver, Daniel Major-Smith, Benjamin Grant Purzycki
articles.abstract: Religious beliefs regarding deities’ punishment and knowledge breadth may facilitate the expansion of cooperative behaviour towards distant co-religionists. However, most previous research in this area has targeted so-called “moralistic” religious traditions (e.g., Christianity, Buddhism), and thus the extent to which these findings apply to other religious traditions is unclear. Here, we aimed to explore this question among the Kichwa, an indigenous group in Amazonian Ecuador, focusing on religious traditions regarding a pan-Quechua nature spirit, Pachamama. Our estimand of interest was the joint causal effect of beliefs about Pachamama’s punishment and omniscience on cooperation with distant Pachamama co-religionists, with cooperation assessed via two experimental games (the Random Allocation Game and Dictator Game). Across all models and sensitivity analyses to different causal assumptions regarding the structure of confounding, we found little evidence for a relationship — either positive or negative — between Pachamama beliefs and cooperation. Assuming a causal interpretation, these results suggest that, in this population, beliefs regarding local deities do not motivate cooperative behaviour towards distant co-religionists.

articles.title: The mediating role of school absenteeism in the socioeconomic achievement gap
articles.authors: Jascha Dräger, Markus Klein, Edward Sosu
articles.abstract: Socioeconomic disparities in children's academic achievement are well established, but the underlying mechanisms are less well understood. We test the hypothesis that socioeconomic disparities in children’s achievement in early primary school are partially mediated via school absences. We used linked data from the Millennium Cohort Study (MCS, birth cohort 2000/01) and the National Pupil Database (NPD) and applied a regression-with-residuals approach to examine the extent to which school absences in school Years 1 and 2 mediate the association between socio-economic status (SES) and achievement at the end of school year 2 in England (N=8,024). Using different achievement measures (reading, writing, math, science, and average) and a broad socioeconomic disadvantage index (based on parental education, occupational class, income, housing tenure, and neighborhood deprivation), we found that a one standard deviation increase in SES is associated with 0.37 to 0.43 standard deviations higher achievement in early primary school. Accounting for a rich set of mediator-outcome confounders, school attendance significantly explained (6.45 to 8.10%) the link between the broad socioeconomic disadvantage index and achievement. Similar results are obtained when measuring SES with household income. Additional analyses show that absences in later years mediate the SES-achievement gaps in Years 6 and 11. Our findings suggest that improving attendance will help reduce the SES-achievement gap.

articles.title: Between Invisible and Illegitimate: How the DSA ‘sees’ Resistance to Content Moderation
articles.abstract: For as long as platforms have been moderating people’s online participation they, in turn, have found ways to resist such sociotechnical governance. This paper offers two contributions to the field of platform governance through consolidating and mapping the sociological literature on these different practices of resistance and, subsequently, systematizing their legal classifications under the EU Digital Services Act (DSA) in a typology. Deepening the understanding of this relationship between resistance and the legal framework will help in delineating the types of conflicts the DSA can(not) address and what space it leaves people to resist moderation by means outside the law, which is especially relevant in the context of rising platform illiberalism. The paper illuminates how the DSA is largely unable to ‘see’ practices of resistance, disregarding the systemic conditions they respond to, and in the rare instances that it does acknowledge resistance, renders it illegitimate.

articles.title: Centring Resistance: How the EU Law and Political Economy of Content Moderation Structures Online Participation
articles.abstract: How people resist the governance of their online participation offers vital insight into how power functions on platforms, yet platform governance research has insufficiently recognised the methodological value of these practices. Grounded in radical democratic theory, this paper argues that centring concrete instances of resistance as entry points uniquely illuminates the legal and political-economic structures of content moderation. The paper develops a theoretical framework positioning practices of resistance not as mere technical workarounds but as democratic sites of contestation that reveal how power circulates within content moderation systems. Drawing on standpoint epistemology and Tully's conception of 'practices of freedom', it argues that the experiences of those subjected to platform governance hold privileged epistemic value, particularly given the restrictive access to platform data that limits other modes of inquiry. This framework is applied through three vignettes centring Black women, fat women, and sex workers who develop strategies of circumvention, challenge, and refusal in response to moderation systems that systematically exclude them. Each vignette uses a specific practice of resistance as an entry point to illuminate a distinct dimension of the political economy of content moderation: the recession of contestable sites into opaque algorithmic systems; the collective visibility generated by circumvention practices such as algospeak; and the structural limits of resistance shaped by platforms' advertiser-driven business models and the EU's Digital Services Act. The paper concludes that centring resistance as a method is essential for understanding how legal and political-economic power operates in online speech governance.

articles.title: Prices in Different Modes of Sale: Theory and RCT Evidence from Forced Sales
articles.authors: Matz Dahlberg, Mikael Lundholm, Mattias Nordin, Henk Schouten
articles.abstract: The mode of sale may affect prices not only through selling rules, but also by changing the composition of potential buyers. We study this mechanism by randomly varying the mode of sale in a three-year field experiment, conducted with the Swedish Enforcement Authority, that assigns foreclosed homes to auction or brokered sale. Assignment to brokered sales raise prices by about 15% relative to auctions. Using pre-treatment appraisals, we show that the foreclosure discount is mode-dependent: auction sales occur at a substantial discount, whereas brokered sales occur close to appraised market value. To explain why the discount arises in auctions, we develop a model in which auction participation barriers deter household buyers and create scope for resale-oriented professional buyers, lowering prices and potentially generating inefficient allocations. Historical individual-level foreclosure data support the mechanism: repeat buyers are present in auctions, purchase at larger discounts, and resell more quickly than other buyers.

articles.title: Agent-based models of social media: A scoping review and research agenda
articles.authors: Aytalina Kulichkina, Daisuke Nakamura, Taehee Kim, Jula Lühring, David Garcia, Dongyoung Sohn, Annie Waldherr
articles.abstract: Social media research has advanced our understanding of individual-level processes in online communication, yet these insights are rarely linked to meso- and macro-level outcomes relevant to the field, including polarization dynamics, misinformation spread, and public opinion formation. Agent-based modeling (ABM), as a method of constructing theoretical representations of systems, offers a powerful approach to bridging this gap by linking micro-level interactions to meso-level processes and emergent macro-level phenomena. While ABM has been widely adopted in computer science, engineering, mathematics, and economics to model social media communication, such applications often rely on theoretical assumptions and empirical findings outside of social media research. To address this disconnect and encourage broader adoption of ABM in the field, we systematically review how the method has been applied across various disciplines to study social media phenomena and offer a comprehensive foundation for building on existing models. We identify common practices, research gaps, and opportunities for theoretical, methodological, and open science advancements. The resulting agenda provides a practical entry point for re-searchers new to modeling and recommends grounding relevant models in established theories and insights from social media research.

articles.title: Effect of financial satisfaction on life satisfaction among older adults in Finland
articles.abstract: Objectives. As populations age, it is increasingly important to understand the factors that shape life satisfaction among older adults. Financial security is widely considered one of the major life domains that contributes to life satisfaction. However, there is a scarcity of longitudinal studies examining how financial satisfaction affects overall life satisfaction among older adults. Previous analyses have mainly focused on cross-sectional associations rather than causal designs. Methods. This study addresses several existing research gaps by using longitudinal survey data on Finnish old-age pensioners aged 64 or over. Fixed effects linear regression models are used to help isolate the effect of financial satisfaction. A sensitivity analysis is conducted to determine whether unobserved confounders could explain the results. In addition, to contextualize the observed effect of financial satisfaction, we compare that effect with the effects of changes in major life-course events. Results. The study finds that financial satisfaction has a considerable effect on overall life satisfaction. Sensitivity analysis suggests it is unlikely that the effect of financial satisfaction is explained by unobserved confounders, lending further support to the idea that there is a causal relationship. Changes in financial satisfaction are more relevant to life satisfaction than changes in self-rated health. Changes in partnership status or work status had no effect on life satisfaction. Discussion. Overall, the study shows that changes in financial satisfaction influence life satisfaction among older adults. Improving financial security in retirement can therefore be expected to also improve subjective well-being among aging populations.

articles.title: DETERMINANTS AND ECONOMIC IMPACT OF CROP DIVERSIFICATION: A MICRO-LEVEL ANALYSIS OF PRODUCTIVITY, INCOME RESILIENCE, AND CLIMATE ADAPTATION IN AGRARIAN ECONOMIES
articles.abstract: Abstract Indeed, the potential of diversifying crops as a strategy to address these concurrent issues of climate variability, income insecurity, and productivity stagnation in agri-based economies is picking up momentum. The present paper constructs a micro level framework to study determinants and economic consequences of crop diversification in an eastern semi-arid agrarian district in eastern India among small scale farmers, marginal farmers and medium farmers. The study employs a mixed primary - secondary data design with 240 farm households in six villages structured in a crops hierarchy to calculate diversification based on Herfindahl concentration ratio and Simpson Crop Diversification Index. Using descriptive, exploratory statistics, correlation analysis and OLS regression analysis, the analysis cross-references diversification and farm productivity, gross margin per acre, and income resilience, and climate adaptation behaviour. The findings also show that the accessibility of irrigation water, receiving educational information, knowledge of extension officer, and proximity of markets, institutional credit, risk perception and climate change adaptation strategies have significant effects on crop diversification. Households in the high diversification group contribute more crop-equivalents than those in the cereal-concentrated group and enjoy better income returns per acre and reduced income variability. The estimated improvements in farm income and resilience scores are significant between every 0.10 increase in the Simpson diversification index, regardless of the number of other factors adjusted for, such as farm size, input intensity, use of irrigation, and adoption of technology. The rationale behind diversification also points to its ability to be a mechanism for livelihood risk-staking management rather than just a adapting to cropping patterns, particularly under climate shocks manifested in mono-cropping rice-wheat systems. The paper makes a case for shifting focus from area substitution to integrated policy support for water security, water extension, local value chains, water aggregation/drying, price assurance and farmer-producer institutional support. Results are relevant to sustainable inclusive agricultural development, climate resilient rural livelihoods and provision of farmers/agricultural policy at regional level in India. Keywords: Crop diversification; farm income; climate adaptation; productivity; smallholders; Herfindahl index; Simpson index; India JEL Codes: Q12; Q15; Q18; Q54; O13; C31

articles.title: Unmoved by the Machine: Stability in Finnish Adolescents’ Work-Life Expectations Through the Generative-AI Transition, 2021-2026
articles.abstract: The public release of ChatGPT in November 2022 and the subsequent escalation of future of-work discourse have been accompanied by forecasts that a large share of cognitive work is exposed to large language models. If adolescents form occupational expectations partly in response to the perceived future of work, this period should register as a shock to those ex pectations. Using seven annual waves of a large repeated cross-sectional survey of Finnish lower- and upper-secondary students (the Nuorten tulevaisuusraportti; 20202026, n ≈ 4,900 6,400 per wave; the core work-expectation battery is observed from 2021), we document the opposite: aggregate work-life self-expectations are remarkably stable across the generative-AI transition. Belief in nding one’s place in working life moved within an eight-point band (68 76%) across the observed 20212026 waves; eagerness for working life and felt occupational competence were e ectively at; the ranking of attractive elds was nearly unchanged. The few genuine movements a slow rise in entrepreneurial openness and a pre-2022 rise in work aversion predate or run orthogonal to the AI shock. We interpret this stability through three converging mechanisms documented elsewhere: the historical stickiness of teenage aspirations, an invulnerability bias by which disruption is attributed to others’ jobs rather than one’s own, and the muted realized labour-market e ects of generative AI through 20242025. The claim throughout concerns aggregate measured expectations, not subjective AI awareness or anxiety. The nding is descriptive, not causal: the instrument was not designed to measure AI, and within-wave subgroup gaps dwarf any temporal drift. We argue the null is nonetheless informa tive for debates that assume youth expectations track technological headlines.

articles.title: Anthropocene and Empire: Discourse Networks of the Human Record
articles.abstract: Scientists have recently proposed 1610 as a candidate for the first year of the new geological epoch known as the Anthropocene, pinpointing the rise of European colonialism and the global movement of life among ecosystems it precipitated as the triggers for measurable changes in the earth system. Using digital humanities methods to mine historical archives, this essay evaluates the relation between colonialism, early modern globalism, and the origins of the Anthropocene. We argue that the Anthropocene was initiated by a premodern earth system that defined life across the globe in spatial terms, furthering the goals of empire — a regime of biopower that has not been adequately acknowledged in debates on the Anthropocene.

articles.title: Social Media and Digital Politics: Networked Reason in an Age of Digital Emotion
articles.authors: James Jaehoon Lee, Jeffrey Layne Blevins
articles.abstract: Informed by critical theory, this book employs Social Network Analysis (SNA) to examine the ever-increasing impact that social media has on politics and contemporary civic discourse. In just the past decade, social media platforms have been at the forefront of political discord that played out in the January 6th insurrection, the expulsion of a US President from major social media platforms, the attempted regulation of social media in various states, and the takeover of Twitter (now "X") by one of the richest and (arguably) most financially influential persons in the world. This book examines these phenomena through a comprehensive and in-depth exploration of their meaning and implication for democratic society. Informed by SNA, James Jaehoon Lee and Jeffrey Layne Blevins examine several types of social and political commentary on one of the most influential social media networks and argue that the use of emotional appeals in these posts about social and political topics degrades the quality of civic discourse and encourages the abandonment of reasoning in democratic self-governance. A timely and vital text for upper-level students and scholars in a variety of disciplines from media and communication studies, journalism, and digital humanities to social network analysis, political science, and sociology.

articles.title: Cultural Representation on AI-Generated Music Platforms: A Quantitative-Qualitative Study
articles.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) music generation platforms are now widely available worldwide and appear capable of generating music in any language or style describable through text. However, these services are largely developed within Anglo-American cultural contexts, raising questions about how culturally marked differences are represented beyond dominant musical norms. This article examines cultural representation in AI-generated music by focusing on songs associated with Chinese language and cultural references on two major text-to-audio platforms, Suno and Udio. The study adopts qualitative-quantitative approach. Computational text analysis is used to cluster Chinese-language lyrics and analyze user-applied tags, while qualitative musicological case studies examine how these representational patterns are articulated into specific musical instances. The findings show that Chinese cultural references are primarily encoded through a limited set of symbolic markers, such as language labels, traditional instruments, and broad stylistic descriptors. While lyrics frequently draw on classical poetry and religious texts, their sonic realization often relies on globally dominant musical templates, producing hybrid and unstable representations. The article demonstrates that AI music platforms not only reshape the boundaries of musical creation but also actively intervene in the reproduction of cultural representation on a global scale. It contributes a methodological framework for empirically examining cultural representation in AI-generated music and highlights the value of interdisciplinary collaboration between musicology and data science.

articles.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of Cross-League Performance Translation in Elite Football
articles.abstract: Predicting footballer performance following cross-league transfers is challenging when statistics derive from distinct tactical and competitive environments. We introduce a hierarchical Bayesian framework for quantifying league translation effects separately for attacking/midfield and defensive player roles. Using bridge datasets of 174 attacking and midfield transfers and 106 defensive transfers from the four major European feeder leagues (La Liga, Bundesliga, Serie A, Ligue 1) and within-Premier League transfers as a domestic baseline (2017/18–2022/23), we fit joint multivariate Bayesian models decomposing translation effects into league, team, and age components while capturing metric correlations via LKJ covariance structures. The primary contribution is calibrated uncertainty quantification: 90% highest density intervals enabling principled probabilistic reasoning in recruitment decision-making. Validation on two held-out, strictly cross-league transfer cohorts (attacking and midfield n = 45, defensive n = 35) demonstrates near-nominal interval coverage on attacking metrics and excellent convergence diagnostics (zero divergences, R̂ ≤ 1.005, where R̂ is the Gelman–Rubin potential scale reduction factor). Results reveal asymmetric league effects: La Liga shows a credibly positive shot-creating actions association (posterior mean +0.306, 90% HDI [+0.015, +0.578]). The framework is currently restricted to Premier League destinations; extending to multi-destination prediction remains an important avenue for future work.

articles.title: Sustainable Agricultural Livelihoods in Tribal Odisha: An Analysis of Indigenous Farming Systems, Market Access, and Socio-Economic Challenges
articles.abstract: Because tribal agriculture in Odisha is located at the intersection of rainfed farming, forest dependence, smallholder risk and policy experimentation including in millets, women’s collectives, farmer producer organisations, forest rights, and decentralized procurement. This article examines the sustainability of livelihoods in tribal communities resulting from indigenous farming systems, which is related to market-access and socio-economic constraints affecting livelihood security. The analysis uses secondary data and considers government statistics, peer-reviewed literature, institutional reports, and policy documents using the Sustainable Livelihoods Framework and theories around agroecology, indigenous knowledge systems, and market access. The evidence demonstrates that mixed cropping, millet-pulse systems, low-external-input maintenance of landrace seeds, pest and soil management, livestock integration, forest-food systems, among others, enhance natural and social capital, facilitate dietary diversification of house barn, and build ecological resilience at rainfed and upland areas. Despite this potential, a number of factors limit their livelihoods, including: small and fragmented holdings, uncertainty in rainfall, land and forest tenure insecurity, limited irrigation, weak rural roads, inadequate storage and processing, dependence on intermediaries, poor access to institutional credit, gendered drudgery, and inadequate access to extension services. The Odisha Millets Mission/Shree Anna Abhiyan, Agriculture Production Cluster model, self-help groups, tribal livelihood programmes and other recent interventions have facilitated better coordination, aggregation and price discovery in selected locations. However, impact of these interventions is contingent upon last mile infrastructure, procurement timing, working capital, participatory extension and culturally compatible technology. Sustainable tribal agriculture will require more than the promotion of crops. It will require rights-based governance of resources, resilient markets, women-centred skill-saving innovations, community seed systems, climate adaptation, and institutional convergence. The framework of integrated livelihood-market for tribal agrarian development in Odisha has been contributed through the study along with highlighting priorities for future field-based and longitudinal research.
